response.justification: The candidate brings 17+ years of genuine engineering breadth — high-throughput distributed systems, embedded, mobile, full-stack — and recent hands-on work with AI agent infrastructure and MCP frameworks at the current role. Founding and sustaining an open-source geo-data platform and speaking at technical conferences show the builder mindset and knowledge-sharing instinct the role values. However, the position is a director-level individual contributor expected to advise strategic customers, shape cloud product futures, and be a recognized voice in AI. The AI work on the resume is recent, briefly described, and lacks quantified outcomes; cloud expertise surfaces only as incidental tooling (AWS CDK); and there is no evidence of executive-level advisory relationships, roadmap ownership, or industry recognition in AI — the markers that distinguish a Principal/AI-Strategy IC from a strong senior engineer.
strengths: 17+ years of continuous hands-on engineering across distributed systems, embedded, mobile, and full-stack domains demonstrates the experience floor the role requires.
Current role explicitly includes AI-agent infrastructure — MCP code generators and agentic workflow frameworks — giving directly relevant, production-context AI exposure.
Founder of GISCollective and recurring workshop/conference speaker establish an entrepreneurial track record and the ability to inspire and educate others technically.
concerns: AI expertise is recent and lightly substantiated: a single role with two sentences and no measurable outcomes falls well short of 'recognized expertise with demonstrated AI products or platforms at scale.'
No cloud architecture depth is evident; AWS CDK appears as a peripheral tool in one role rather than as a cloud platform design competency.

No demonstrated strategic advisory experience — advising enterprise customers, influencing product roadmaps at organizational scale, or establishing industry recognition in AI — which are core to the director-level scope of this role.
